Google just dropped something that changes everything. I'm talking about a tool that builds full app interfaces from a single prompt. No designer, no developer, no waiting weeks, just you, a description, and a finished product ready to go live. And the new update just made it 10 times more powerful because now AI agents can use it automatically without you even touching it. Your entire front-end workflow could literally run on autopilot. And I'm not talking about some far-off future thing. This is available right now. Designers used to be the bottleneck. Developers used to be the bottleneck. That bottleneck is gone. The way apps and websites get built just changed forever. And the businesses that figure this out early are going to have a massive advantage over everyone still doing things the old way. I'm going to break down exactly what dropped, why it matters, show you real workflows you can steal, and give you a real example of how this works for an actual business. By the end of this video, you'll know exactly how to use this. Stay with me. Let's go. Hey, if we haven't met already, I'm the digital avatar of Julian Goldie, CEO of SEO agency Goldie Agency. Whilst he's helping clients get more leads and customers, I'm here to help you get the latest AI updates. Julian Goldie reads every comment, so make sure you comment below. Let's start simple. Google Stitch is an AI powered UI generator. You describe what you want and it builds the full interface for you. Buttons, layout, screens, navigation, the whole thing. And it doesn't just give you a picture, it gives you real working code, HTML, CSS, Tailwind, React Ready components, and even Figma designs. All powered by Gemini 2.5, Google's most capable AI model right now. So, the basic loop looks like this. You type a prompt. Stitch understands the layout, the UX, the structure, it builds the interface, it hands you the code. Done. What used to take a designer days and a developer, even longer now, takes minutes. That alone is already a big deal. But what just dropped is a whole different level. Here's what changed. Google just launched the official TypeScript SDK for Stitch. One line to install it. MPM II Google Stitch SDK. And now Stitch is fully programmable. You can embed it into your own apps, your own workflows, your own systems. You can tell it to create a project, edit a screen, list available tools, all from code. No manual clicking, no UI needed. But the real headline is the agent first API. This is the part most people are completely sleeping on because what this means is that AI agents can now use Stitch as a tool, not you, the agent. An AI workflow can now call Stitch, tell it to build something and move on. You set the goal, the agent handles the rest. Design happens automatically. Code gets generated automatically, all without you touching anything. This is not a small update. This is a completely different way of building digital products. Let me make this concrete. Imagine you're building something for the AI profit boardroom, which is an AI automation community built to help business owners use AI tools to work smarter and grow faster. You want a new landing page, one that explains clearly what the AI profit boardroom is, shows the value of AI automation, and gets people to take action and join the community. In the old world, you'd brief a designer, wait several days, review it, request changes, wait again, then hand it to a developer, wait some more, and finally go live. That whole process could take two or 3 weeks easily. Here's how it looks with Stitch. You open the tool and type something like, "Build a high converting landing page for the AI profit boardroom. The page should explain the benefits of AI automation for business owners. Highlight why the community is valuable and make it easy for visitors to sign up. You hit enter. Stitch generates the layout, the section structure, the button placements, the visual hierarchy. You export the code. You're live. The whole thing takes minutes, not weeks. And now with the new SDK and agent API, you don't even have to type that prompt yourself. You can build a system that does it automatically. Tools like Stitch are powerful, but tools alone don't build a business. Strategy does. Systems do. Knowing which tools to use, when to use them, and how to connect them into a real workflow. That's what moves the needle. That's exactly what the AI profit boardroom is built for. It's a community of business owners and entrepreneurs who are using AI automation to work smarter. Not just collecting tools, actually implementing them, building real systems, saving real time. If you're watching this and you want to go beyond just knowing about tools like Stitch and actually start using them in a structured way, the AI profit boardroom is where that happens. Link is in the description. Go check it out. Let's go deeper on the agent first architecture because this is genuinely new ground with the agent API. Stitch exposes tools that an AI agent can call directly. Things like create project, edit screen, list tools. So now you can build automated workflows where an AI agent is the one making design decisions and generating interfaces on demand. Think about what that makes possible for an online business. A new lead comes into your funnel. An AI agent checks what kind of business they run. That same agent calls Stitch and generates a custom onboarding dashboard personalized to their niche. Then it emails them the link. You didn't do a single thing. The agent handled the design, the generation, and the delivery. Fully automated, fully personalized. Or imagine running five different landing page variations to test which message resonates best with your audience. Old approach, five designers, significant budget, weeks of back and forth. New approach, one agent, five prompts, five pages, built by Stitch, done in under an hour. That's what autonomous UI generation looks like in practice. Let's be straight. Stitch is not a magic wand. It's still experimental in some areas. It doesn't handle complex app logic, authentication systems, or database architecture. For those, you still need a developer. The quality of what you get out depends heavily on the quality of your prompt. Vague prompts produce vague results, and it works best for interfaces, landing pages, dashboards, and onboarding flows rather than full stack applications. But within those boundaries, it's already incredibly capable. And the trajectory it's on suggests those boundaries are going to keep expanding. The standard workflow for building something digital is changing fast. Design and development are merging. AI agents are handling more of the manual work. The people who win are the ones who learn to direct these tools well, not the ones who ignore them. Designers won't disappear. But the role shifts. You stop being the person who manually moves pixels. You become the person who knows how to describe great work to an AI and get it back in seconds. That skill, knowing how to prompt, how to direct, how to orchestrate AI tools like Stitch inside a real business workflow, that's the skill worth building right now. Google Stitch turns prompts into full UI and working code. The new update brings a TypeScript SDK, a fully programmable API, and an agent first architecture that lets AI workflows build interfaces automatically. The three workflows to start with are the lead magnet page builder, the automated onboarding dashboard, and the AB page testing machine. The bottleneck between design and development is gone. And the businesses that figure out how to use tools like this inside real systems are going to move a lot faster than everyone else.
